query: biochemical pathway of epoxyeicosatrienoic acids production from arachidonic acid

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: The second group of mediators consists of arachidonic acid mediators. Arachidonic acid is a phospholipid that constitutes the membrane of the body's cells. When activated by inducers such as infection or tissue damage, the enzyme phospholipase can act on the membrane phospholipids to liberate the arachidonic acid. This component can potentially metabolize into 2 main components through either the cyclooxygenase pathway or the 5-lipooxygenase pathway. The cyclooxygenase pathway forms the prostaglandin (PG) mediators, including prostaglandin D2 (PGD2) and thromboxane (which are the bronchoconstrictive prostaglandins), and the bronchoprotective or inhibitory PGE2 and prostacyclin. On the other hand, the 5-lipooxygenase pathway forms the leukotrienes (LTs). Examples of leukotrienes include LTB4, which is neutrophil adhesive and chemotactic, LTC4, D4, and E4, which are involved in the contraction of smooth muscle bronchioles, vasoconstriction, and edema formation. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Ibuprofen -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The primary mechanism of ibuprofen involves inhibiting prostaglandin precursors. After a physiological or pathological stimulus, membrane phospholipids release arachidonic acid through the actions of the enzyme phospholipase A2. Arachidonic acid is fed into 1 of 3 enzymatic pathways: cyclooxygenase (COX), lipoxygenase (LOX), and cytochrome P450 (CYP450). The cyclooxygenase pathway converts arachidonic acid to prostaglandins, prostacyclins, and thromboxanes. The lipoxygenase pathway yields hydroxy-eicosatetraenoic acids (HETEs), leukotrienes, and lipoxins. Lastly, the cytochrome P450 pathway converts arachidonic acid to HETEs and epoxyeicosatrienoic acids (EET). [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The induction of the arachidonic acid pathway via inflammatory mediators is well-described: phospholipases (especially A2) cleave arachidonic acid from sources including the cellular membrane, and arachidonic acid is then acted on by cyclo-oxygenase enzymes that convert arachidonic acid to prostaglandins which enter circulation. [4] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Leukotrienes -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Leukotrienes undergo synthesis via the 5-lipoxygenase pathway of arachidonic acid metabolism. Arachidonic acid is a fatty acid found within the phospholipids that constitute cell membranes. When a stimulus arrives that calls for the production of leukotrienes, phospholipids within our cell membranes metabolize to arachidonic acid via phospholipase A (PLA). The arachidonic acid is then acted on by 5-lipooxygenase in concert with FLAP to yield LTA. Within the cell, LTA is used as the substrate for the production of LTB via hydrolysis or leukotriene C (LTC) via conjugation with glutathione. At this point, LTB and LTC are exported out of the cell via separate transport proteins. Extracellularly, LTC can subsequently undergo hydrolysis to produce leukotriene (LTE). LTC, LTD (an intermediate in LTE synthesis), and LTE are termed cysteinyl leukotrienes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry of Platelet Activating Factor -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Products of phosphatidylinositol metabolism, diacylglycerol (DAG), and inositol triphosphate (IP3) enhance the action of protein kinase C (PKC). DAG directly stimulates PKC and IP3 indirectly through calcium by opening intracellular calcium channels in the endoplasmic reticulum. PKC activation leads to many responses, such as the release of arachidonic acid, eicosanoid production, and phosphorylation of protein tyrosine. Metabolites of arachidonic acids can also open membrane calcium channels. In addition to activating PKC, intracellular calcium can also activate intracellular contractile proteins, phospholipase A2 (PLA2), and many other proteins as well. Activation of PLA2 results in the synthesis of prostaglandins, thromboxane A2, leukotrienes, and other inflammatory mediators. These inflammatory mediators are involved in leukocyte activation, chemotaxis, aggregation of cells and platelets, and generation of reactive oxygen species. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry of Platelet Activating Factor -- Fundamentals -- Biosynthesis
retrieved_docs.content: Biosynthesis through the remodeling pathway has two steps, and it contributes to the bulk of the synthesis of PAF during inflammation. In the first step, phospholipase A2 (PLA2) acts on phosphatidylcholine producing eicosanoid (arachidonic acid) and lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC). In the second and final step, acetyl residue is transferred to LPC by LPC acetyltransferase to produce PAF.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: All prostanoids share a common biosynthetic pathway. Phospholipase A releases arachidonic acid from the phospholipid membrane, after which cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes and their bound synthase enzymes process the substrate into the various prostanoids. The prostanoid produced depends on metabolism by COX-1 or COX-2, which differentially express in tissues, and the respective synthase enzymes they bind. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry of Platelet Activating Factor -- Fundamentals -- Biosynthesis
retrieved_docs.content: There are two pathways to synthesize PAF: Remodeling and de-novo synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fatty Acid Oxidation -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Omega-oxidation of fatty acids in the endoplasmic reticulum primarily functions to hydroxylate and oxidize fatty acids to dicarboxylic acids to increase water solubility for excretion in the urine. This enzymatic conversion relies on the cytochrome P450 superfamily to catalyze this reaction between xenobiotic compounds and molecular oxygen. [9] Deficiencies in some enzymes of fatty acid oxidation may result in accumulation. Thus, up-regulation of omega-oxidation, increased serum, and or urine medium-chain dicarboxylic acids can be diagnostic of certain deficiencies and will be discussed under the “Clinical Significance” section.

retrieved_docs.title: Salicylic Acid (Aspirin) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Researchers hypothesize that due to the blocking of the COX pathway, the arachidonic acids are shuttled into the lipoxygenase pathway. The production of anti-inflammatory lipoxins results from modifying prostaglandin-endoperoxide synthase (PTGS2), also called COX-2, that results in the production of lipoxins, most of which are anti-inflammatory. These compounds are called aspirin-triggered lipoxins, aspirin-triggered resolvins, and aspirin-triggered maresins.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Thromboxane A2 -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The production of TxA2 is via a sequential process that begins with arachidonic acid (AA). Arachidonic acid is a polyunsaturated fatty acid present in the phospholipid membrane of the body’s cells. In the setting of inflammation, phospholipase A2 cleaves arachidonic acid from the cell’s membrane and, through one pathway, is converted to prostaglandin H2 (PGH2) via the action of the enzyme COX. There are 2 evolutionarily conserved COX isoforms, including COX-1 and COX-2. COX-1 is constitutively expressed in platelets, and COX-2 is an inducible form found only in a small fraction of circulating platelets under normal conditions. COX-2 is shown to have increased expression in newly formed platelets and during times of inflammation and other provoked settings compared to normal conditions. PGH2 ultimately converts into the product TxA2 via the enzyme TXAS. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The main indication for leukotriene receptor antagonists is in treating chronic asthma. Leukotrienes are synthesized from arachidonic acid by the action of 5-lipoxygenase in many inflammatory cells in the airways. [9] Arachidonic acid is released from cell membrane phospholipids mainly by phospholipase A2. [10] The cyclooxygenase pathway produces thromboxane and prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. Corticosteroids inhibit phospholipase A2 and subsequent synthesis of eicosanoid inflammatory mediators, including both prostaglandins and leukotrienes. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs such as aspirin inhibit cyclooxygenases. Zileuton inhibits 5-Lipoxygenase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fatty Acid Oxidation -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: As previously mentioned, omega-oxidation, the third and final fatty acid oxidation pathway, occurs in the endoplasmic reticulum. This pathway exists to process large, water-insoluble fatty acids that would otherwise be toxic to the cell in higher concentrations. Further details and mechanisms of these accessory pathways/enzymes to beta-oxidation are discussed below under the heading “Molecular.”

retrieved_docs.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: Omega-3 fatty acids undergo metabolism and oxidation in the liver, leading to the synthesis of VLDL to transport fatty acids in the plasma to various tissues. Omega-3 fatty acids-generated lipid signaling molecules are catalyzed by cyclooxygenase and lipooxygenase enzymes. While most DHA and EPA metabolism occurs via beta-oxidation in the liver (as discussed above), cytochrome P450 (CYP)-mediated metabolism is a minor pathway in the breakdown of DHA and EPA. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Latanoprost -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin synthesis occurs in the cell from arachidonic acid. Arachidonic acid, a 20-carbon molecule, gets metabolized to leukotrienes (LT) by lipoxygenases or converted to cyclic endoperoxides by cyclooxygenases. The cyclic endoperoxides are metabolized to prostaglandin F2-alpha (PGF2α) by prostaglandin synthase, reductase, and thromboxanes by thromboxane synthase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Phospholipase A2 -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: PLA plays several important physiologic roles, including the production of inflammatory eicosanoid compounds from arachidonic acid. These compounds are involved in the pathogenesis of various inflammatory conditions, including rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, multiple forms of cancer, cardiovascular disease, and other inflammatory diseases. The advent of pharmacological compounds that function as PLA inhibitors serves an exciting role in our future approach to treating various inflammatory conditions. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins (prostaginy) are biologically active lipid mediators that serve as key components in prostaglandin measurement assays, where their levels are quantified for diagnostic purposes. They are classified within the broader group of hormones, their metabolites, and precursors, underscoring their role as hormone‑like signaling molecules. Prostaglandins also belong to the eicosanoid family (ikosanoidy), representing a specific subclass of these polyunsaturated fatty‑acid derivatives. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly linked to prostaglandin biosynthesis, establishing a functional relationship between the enzyme and prostaglandin production. Several therapeutic drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary tract disorders are derived from or based on prostaglandins, reflecting their clinical utility in managing uterine contractility, cervical ripening, and smooth‑muscle tone. Isopropyl unoprostone is identified as having a structural class of prostaglandins, exemplifying a synthetic analog that mimics prostaglandin activity. Additionally, prostaglandin endoperoxides (prostaglandinové endoperoxidy) are recognized as downstream products—or “children”—of prostaglandins within the metabolic pathway. Collectively, these interconnections highlight the central role of prostaglandins in physiology, pharmacology, and diagnostic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2562
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny serves as a central prostaglandin‑related entity that links a variety of clinical, biochemical and pharmacological concepts. In the context of prostaglandin measurement, prostaglandiny is identified as a component of the assay, highlighting its relevance to laboratory evaluation. Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under prostaglandiny as a parent category, indicating that many agents in these specialties belong to this prostaglandin family. Likewise, hormones and their metabolites and precursors are organized under prostaglandiny as a parent group, reflecting the role of prostaglandins as hormonal mediators and metabolic intermediates. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (noted as cyklooxygenasy) has a direct relationship with prostaglandiny, underscoring its essential function in the biosynthesis of prostaglandins from fatty acid precursors. Specific prostaglandin analogues such as epoprostenol and prostaglandin A1 are described as children of prostaglandiny, indicating that they are individual compounds derived from or belonging to this broader class. Ikosikanoidy is linked as a parent of prostaglandiny, suggesting a higher‑order classification that encompasses prostaglandiny within a larger lipid‑mediator family. Finally, isopropyl unoprostone is noted to have the structural class of prostaglandiny, placing this molecule within the same chemical framework. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandiny integrates measurement techniques, therapeutic drug groups, hormonal pathways, enzymatic synthesis, and distinct prostaglandin compounds, providing a cohesive framework for understanding prostaglandin biology and its applications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 946
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny, representing the family of prostaglandins, are biologically active lipid compounds that are measured in clinical prostaglandin measurement assays as a key component. They belong to the broader eicosanoid class, reflecting their origin from arachidonic acid metabolism, and are closely linked to the cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymatic pathway, which governs their synthesis. In therapeutic practice, prostaglandiny serve as the parent class for several drug groups that are primarily employed in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, underscoring their importance in managing conditions such as labor induction, cervical ripening, and bladder dysfunction. Specific pharmaceutical agents—including epoprostenol, isopropyl unoprostone, and the Prostaglandin Analog [EPC]—are classified within the structural class of prostaglandiny, indicating that they are designed to mimic or modify the activity of natural prostaglandins. These analogs are used to treat pulmonary hypertension, glaucoma, and other conditions where modulation of prostaglandin pathways is beneficial. Together, the measurement, biochemical relationships, and therapeutic applications of prostaglandiny illustrate their central role in both physiological regulation and targeted medical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 271
retrieved_docs.content: Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under the prostaglandin family, indicating that these therapeutic agents share the core biochemical characteristics of prostaglandins. The broader category “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES” also lists prostaglandins as a parent group, reflecting the role of prostaglandins as hormone‑like mediators that interact with specific receptors and influence physiological processes. Cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly linked to prostaglandins, as this enzyme catalyzes the conversion of arachidonic acid into prostaglandin precursors, underscoring its central function in prostaglandin biosynthesis. The term ikosanoidy (eicosanoids) is identified as a parent class of prostaglandins, situating prostaglandins within the larger family of lipid mediators derived from 20‑carbon fatty acids. Isopropyl unoprostone is described as having the structural class of prostaglandins, meaning it is a synthetic analog that mimics the chemical framework of natural prostaglandins and is used for its pharmacologic effects. Conversely, prostaglandins themselves are noted to have the structural class travoprost, indicating that travoprost is a specific prostaglandin analog that shares the characteristic prostaglandin scaffold. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandins serve as a central hub linking enzymatic pathways, broader lipid mediator families, therapeutic drug groups, and synthetic analogs used in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 906
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin measurement includes prostaglandiny as a component. The drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under prostaglandiny, indicating that these therapeutic agents act as or target prostaglandins. Within the broader taxonomy, prostaglandiny are placed under the category HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES, reflecting their role as hormone‑like signaling molecules. Biochemically, prostaglandiny belong to the eicosanoid family (ikosikanoidy), and their synthesis is closely linked to the enzyme cyklooxygenasy, which catalyzes the formation of prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. Additionally, isopropyl unoprostone is described as having the structural class of prostaglandiny, illustrating that it shares the characteristic prostaglandin backbone. Together, these relationships underscore the central position of prostaglandiny in physiology, pharmacology, and biochemical pathways relevant to obstetric, gynaecologic, and urinary tract therapeutics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1426
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny are lipid mediators that belong to the broader category of HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES. They are synthesized through the enzymatic action of cyklooxygenasy, which converts arachidonic acid into these bioactive compounds. Prostaglandiny are also members of the ikosanoidy family, indicating that they are a type of eicosanoid. Within the prostaglandiny structural class, unoprostone is recognized as a prostaglandin analogue, and isopropyl unoprostone represents a further structural variant derived from unoprostone. Together, these relationships illustrate that prostaglandiny serve as a parent class for specific prostaglandin derivatives such as unoprostone and its isopropyl form, linking enzymatic synthesis, biochemical classification, and structural analogues in a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1100
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny represents a core class of prostaglandin compounds that are integral to several physiological and therapeutic contexts. It is a component measured in prostaglandin measurement assays, reflecting its importance in clinical laboratory evaluation. As a member of the broader eicosanoid family, prostaglandiny is linked to cyclooxygenase activity, which mediates its biosynthesis from fatty‑acid precursors. The category of prostaglandiny also serves as a parent classification for drug groups primarily employed in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, underscoring its therapeutic relevance in reproductive and urological medicine. Additionally, hormones, their metabolites, and precursors are organized under prostaglandiny, highlighting its role in endocrine pathways. Within this structural class, specific analogues such as isopropyl unoprostone are identified as having prostaglandiny as their structural basis, while tafluprost is recognized as a prostaglandin‑derived compound that belongs to the prostaglandiny class. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandiny functions as a central biochemical and pharmacological entity linking measurement techniques, biosynthetic enzymes, hormonal metabolism, and targeted drug therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny is presented as a broad parent category that links a variety of related medical entities. Prostaglandin measurement includes prostaglandiny as a component, indicating that assays of prostaglandins are directly tied to this class. Several therapeutic groups are organized under prostaglandiny, notably drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders, which are classified as having prostaglandiny as their parent. Likewise, the categories “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES” and “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors” are both described as having prostaglandiny as a parent, reflecting the role of prostaglandins within broader hormone and vitamin signaling pathways. The eicosanoid family (ikosikanoidy) is also placed under prostaglandiny, underscoring that prostaglandins are a subclass of eicosanoids. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is noted to have a relationship with prostaglandiny, consistent with its central function in the biosynthesis of prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. Finally, the specific compound isopropyl unoprostone is identified as belonging to the structural class prostaglandiny, illustrating that individual prostaglandin analogues are categorized within this same parent group. Together, these connections highlight prostaglandiny as a unifying concept that integrates measurement techniques, pharmacologic groups, hormonal and metabolic pathways, enzymatic synthesis, and structural classification of prostaglandin-related substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 303
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny serves as a broad parent category that links together several distinct medical entities. Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under prostaglandiny, indicating that these therapeutic agents share a common mechanistic or structural relationship with prostaglandin pathways. The grouping “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES” also falls under prostaglandiny, reflecting the involvement of prostaglandin biology in hormonal regulation, vitamin metabolism and receptor signaling. Likewise, “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors” are placed within the prostaglandiny hierarchy, underscoring the biochemical connections between prostaglandins and the synthesis or breakdown of various endocrine compounds. The term “ikosikanoidy” is similarly identified as a child of prostaglandiny, suggesting that this class of compounds is structurally or functionally related to prostaglandins. The compound isopropyl unoprostone is described as having the structural class of prostaglandiny, indicating that its molecular framework is derived from or resembles prostaglandin structures. Finally, cyclooxygenase (noted as “cyklooxygenasy”) is reported to have a relationship with prostaglandiny, highlighting the enzyme’s pivotal role in the biosynthesis of prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. Together, these connections illustrate how prostaglandiny integrates diverse drug groups, hormonal systems, related metabolites, specific chemical classes, and key enzymatic pathways, providing a unified framework for understanding their interrelated roles in physiology and therapeutic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny represent a central class of lipid mediators that function as the parent category for several therapeutic groupings, including drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders and the broader classification HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES. As a structural class, prostaglandiny encompass prostaglandin analogs such as Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] and isopropyl unoprostone, illustrating how specific synthetic agents are derived from the prostaglandin scaffold. In the larger biochemical hierarchy, prostaglandiny are a subclass of ikosanoidy (eicosanoids), linking them to the extensive family of arachidonic‑acid‑derived signaling molecules. This integrated framework underscores the relevance of prostaglandin‑based compounds across diverse medical specialties and highlights their role within both hormonal/vitamin pathways and targeted pharmacotherapy for obstetric, gynaecologic, and urinary conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1945
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin measurement incorporates prostaglandiny as a key component, reflecting the clinical importance of quantifying these lipid mediators. Prostglandiny serves as the parent class for drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, indicating that many agents in these specialties are prostaglandin‑based therapies. Cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly related to prostaglandiny because it catalyzes the conversion of arachidonic acid into prostaglandins, linking enzyme activity to prostaglandin synthesis. Ikosikanoidy (eicosanoids) is identified as the broader parent category of prostaglandiny, situating prostaglandins within the larger family of eicosanoid signaling molecules. Both isopropyl unoprostone and latanoprost are classified structurally under prostaglandiny, highlighting that these compounds are synthetic prostaglandin analogs used therapeutically, for example, in the management of ocular pressure. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandiny functions as a central biochemical and pharmacological hub, connecting measurement techniques, enzymatic pathways, broader lipid mediator families, and specific drug classes employed across obstetric, gynaecologic, urinary, and ophthalmic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2102
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins (prostaglandiny) are a central biochemical family that underlies a wide range of therapeutic and physiological entities. Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified as having prostaglandins as their parent class, indicating that these medications act through or are derived from prostaglandin pathways. Hormones and their metabolites and precursors also have prostaglandins as a parent, reflecting the role of prostaglandins as key intermediates in hormonal biosynthesis and metabolism. Prostaglandin cardiac therapy preparations are described as having a broader relationship with prostaglandins, signifying that these cardiac drugs are formulated to exploit prostaglandin mechanisms for cardiovascular effects. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is linked to prostaglandins, emphasizing its essential function in the enzymatic conversion of arachidonic acid to prostaglandin compounds. Eicosanoids (ikosikanoidy) are identified as having prostaglandins as a parent, situating prostaglandins within the larger eicosanoid lipid mediator family. Finally, isopropyl unoprostone is noted to have a structural class relationship with prostaglandins, indicating that this compound is a prostaglandin analog used clinically, for example, in the treatment of glaucoma. Together, these connections illustrate how prostaglandins serve as a foundational class for diverse drug groups, hormonal pathways, cardiac therapies, enzymatic processes, and related lipid mediators, highlighting their broad relevance in pharmacology and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1415
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny functions as a central parent class for a variety of related medical entities. The drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under prostaglandiny, indicating that these therapeutic agents are derived from or act upon prostaglandin pathways. The category HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES also has prostaglandiny as its parent, reflecting the involvement of prostaglandins in hormonal regulation, vitamin metabolism and receptor signaling. The enzyme cyklooxygenasy (cyclooxygenase) is described as having a relationship with prostaglandiny, underscoring its key role in the biosynthesis of prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. The subclass ikosikanoidy is placed under prostaglandiny, further expanding the family of prostaglandin‑related compounds. The compound isopropyl unoprostone is identified as having the structural class of prostaglandiny, linking its chemical architecture to the prostaglandin scaffold. Finally, prostaglandin A1 is listed as a child of prostaglandiny, exemplifying a specific individual prostaglandin molecule within the broader family. Together, these connections illustrate how prostaglandiny integrates diverse drug groups, biochemical pathways, structural classes and individual prostaglandins, highlighting its pivotal role in pharmacology, physiology and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: The term “prostaglandiny” (prostaglandins) serves as a central classification for several related medical categories and compounds. Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are organized under the prostaglandin parent class, reflecting their therapeutic reliance on prostaglandin‑like mechanisms. The category “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES” is also placed within the prostaglandin hierarchy, indicating that many hormones, vitamin‑derived mediators and receptor sites involve prostaglandin pathways. Likewise, “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors” are grouped under prostaglandins, emphasizing the role of prostaglandin biosynthesis and metabolism in endocrine function. Prostaglandins themselves are a subclass of the broader eicosanoid family, known in the source language as “ikosanoidy,” situating prostaglandiny within the larger context of lipid‑derived signaling molecules. Within the prostaglandin family, specific members are identified, such as prostaglandin A1, which is listed as a child of prostaglandiny, and isopropyl unoprostone, which is described as having a structural class of prostaglandiny, underscoring its chemical similarity to natural prostaglandins. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandins function as a unifying framework linking diverse drug groups, hormonal systems, metabolic precursors, and individual prostaglandin compounds, all of which are integral to understanding prostaglandin‑mediated physiology and pharmacology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2090
retrieved_docs.content: Peroxides constitute a broad class of reactive oxygen‑containing compounds, and within this class prostaglandin endoperoxides are recognized as a specific subcategory. Prostaglandin endoperoxides are generated through the cyclooxygenase pathway from arachidonic acid and serve as immediate precursors to biologically active prostaglandins and thromboxanes, which mediate inflammation, pain, fever, and platelet aggregation. Recognizing prostaglandin endoperoxides as a “child” of the larger peroxide family clarifies their chemical relationship and underscores their relevance in pharmacologic targeting of inflammatory and thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny are a specific group of lipid mediators that belong to the broader eicosanoid (ikosanoidy) family, reflecting their role as a subclass within this larger class of bioactive compounds. The enzyme cyklooxygenasy is directly related to prostaglandiny, participating in the biochemical pathway that synthesizes these mediators. Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified as having prostaglandiny as their parent, indicating that these therapeutic agents are derived from or act through prostaglandin mechanisms. Additionally, the synthetic compounds isopropyl unoprostone and latanoprost are identified as having a structural class of prostaglandiny, meaning they are prostaglandin analogues employed in clinical practice (for example, latanoprost in the management of glaucoma). Together, these relationships highlight the central importance of prostaglandiny in both physiological signaling and a variety of medical treatments.
